input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system** designed for research and educational purposes. The system comprises:
  - **Physical Crane**: A scaled-down, mechanically actuated gantry crane with **kinematic constraints** (e.g., linear and rotational motion of the hoist and trolley).
  - **Controller**: A dedicated hardware/software controller interfacing with the crane’s actuators (e.g., motors, encoders) to execute motion commands and enforce operational constraints.
  - **Operational Domain**: A **controlled laboratory environment** with predefined motion trajectories (e.g., pick-and-place tasks) and **real-time operational feedback** (e.g., sensor data from position encoders, load cells, or force sensors).
  - **Key Characteristics**:
    - **Modularity**: The crane’s design allows for **reproducible experimentation** with varying payloads, speeds, and kinematic configurations.
    - **Safety-Critical**: Requires **closed-loop control** to prevent collisions, overloading, or unstable motion (e.g., via PID or model-predictive control).
    - **Data-Generative**: Equipped with sensors to log **time-series operational data** (e.g., joint angles, velocities, forces) for validation and fault detection.
    - **Interoperability**: Designed to integrate with **open-source software stacks** (e.g., ROS, MATLAB/Simulink) for seamless digital twin synchronization.
  The system’s **scalability** and **low-cost setup** make it ideal for validating **digital twin synchronization methods**, **fault detection**, and **continuous validation techniques** in a controlled, reproducible manner.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the lab-scale gantry crane provides the following **services**, categorized by functionality and target users (researchers, educators, or system operators):
  - **Optimal Control Services**:
    - **Real-Time Trajectory Optimization**: Computes and enforces **optimal motion profiles** (e.g., minimizing energy consumption or maximizing throughput) using **kinematic/dynamic models** of the crane. Targets **control engineers** and **automation researchers**.
    - **Adaptive Control**: Dynamically adjusts control parameters (e.g., PID gains) based on **real-time sensor feedback** (e.g., load variations, actuator delays) to maintain stability.
  - **Historical Data Logging**:
    - **Time-Series Database**: Stores **structured operational data** (e.g., joint positions, velocities, forces, timestamps) in a **queryable format** (e.g., SQL/NoSQL) for post-mortem analysis. Enables **trend analysis** and **anomaly detection**.
    - **Data Retention Policy**: Supports **long-term archival** of logs for **fault diagnosis** and **performance benchmarking**.
  - **Data Visualization**:
    - **Real-Time Dashboards**: Displays **3D CAD models** (e.g., via **Blender** or **Unity**) synchronized with live sensor data (e.g., hoist position, trolley velocity) for **operator monitoring**.
    - **Comparative Visualization**: Overlays **simulated vs. actual trajectories** to validate **digital twin fidelity** (e.g., using **MATLAB’s Animation Toolbox** or **ROS RViz**).
  - **Continuous Validation**:
    - **Model-Data Consistency Checks**: Compares **digital twin predictions** (e.g., kinematic simulations) with **real-world sensor data** using **statistical methods** (e.g., **residual analysis**, **Kalman filtering**) to detect **divergences** (e.g., due to unmodeled dynamics or sensor noise).
    - **Automated Alerting**: Triggers **warnings** (e.g., via **email/API calls**) when validation metrics exceed thresholds (e.g., **RMSE > 5%**).
  - **Fault Detection**:
    - **Anomaly Detection**: Uses **machine learning models** (e.g., **Isolation Forests**, **LSTM autoencoders**) trained on historical data to identify **deviations** (e.g., sudden torque spikes, position drifts) indicative of **mechanical faults** (e.g., motor wear) or **control failures**.
    - **Root Cause Analysis**: Correlates faults with **specific system components** (e.g., encoder failure, cable slack) via **causal graphs** or **Bayesian networks**.
  - **System Evolution Notation (DarTwin)**:
    - **Versioning Support**: Tracks **changes in the physical/digital system** (e.g., hardware upgrades, model updates) using a **formal notation** to ensure **traceability** of modifications.
    - **Impact Assessment**: Simulates the effects of **hypothetical changes** (e.g., adding a new sensor) on **system performance** before deployment.
  **Target Users**:
  - **Researchers**: Leverage services for **validating synchronization methods**, **testing fault detection algorithms**, or **benchmarking control strategies**.
  - **Educators**: Use **visualization tools** and **historical data** to teach **digital twin concepts** (e.g., model validation, cyber-physical interactions).
  - **Operators**: Access **real-time dashboards** and **alerts** for **proactive maintenance** and **safety monitoring**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** implementation relies on the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their role in the system:
  - **Modeling & Simulation**:
    - **CAD Models**: Created using **open-source/industry-standard tools** (e.g., **FreeCAD**, **SolidWorks**) for **geometric and kinematic representation** of the crane.
    - **Kinematic Model**: Implemented in **MATLAB/Simulink** or **Python (PyBullet, PyRobot)** to simulate **forward/inverse kinematics**, **dynamics**, and **control logic**.
    - **Simulation Engines**: Uses **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for **real-time simulation** and **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing**, enabling **co-simulation** with the physical crane.
  - **Data Acquisition & Streaming**:
    - **Sensor Interfaces**: Integrates with **industrial I/O modules** (e.g., **National Instruments DAQ**) or **microcontroller-based sensors** (e.g., **Arduino**, **Raspberry Pi**) to capture **position (encoders)**, **force (load cells)**, and **velocity (IMUs)** data.
    - **Data Protocols**: Employs **OPC UA**, **MQTT**, or **ROS Topics** for **low-latency, bidirectional communication** between the physical crane and digital twin.
    - **Time Synchronization**: Uses **PTP (Precision Time Protocol)** or **NTP** to align **sensor timestamps** with **digital twin logs** for **sub-millisecond accuracy**.
  - **Control & Optimization**:
    - **Control Algorithms**: Implements **PID controllers**, **model-predictive control (MPC)**, or **reinforcement learning (RL)** via **Python (SciPy, Pyomo)** or **MATLAB**.
    - **Optimization Libraries**: Leverages **CVXPY** or **Gurobi** for **trajectory optimization** (e.g., minimizing energy or time).
  - **Data Storage & Processing**:
    - **Databases**: Stores logs in **SQL (PostgreSQL)** or **NoSQL (MongoDB)** for **scalable querying** and **time-series analysis**.
    - **Stream Processing**: Uses **Apache Kafka** or **ROS Bag** for **real-time data ingestion** and **filtering**.
  - **Visualization & Validation**:
    - **3D Rendering**: Renders **digital twin states** in **Blender**, **Unity**, or **ROS RViz** with **sensor overlays** (e.g., heatmaps for force distribution).
    - **Validation Tools**: Employs **MATLAB’s Statistics Toolbox** or **Python (SciKit-Learn)** for **residual analysis**, **hypothesis testing**, and **fault detection**.
  - **Open-Source Ecosystem**:
    - **Software Stack**: Built on **freely available tools** (e.g., **ROS**, **Python**, **MATLAB’s open-source equivalents**) to ensure **reproducibility** and **low-cost deployment**.
    - **Interoperability**: Supports **standardized interfaces** (e.g., **FMI**, **Function Block Diagrams**) for **third-party integration** (e.g., PLCs, SCADA systems).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Continuous Validation Framework**: Custom-developed **Python scripts** or **MATLAB apps** to automate **model-data comparison** and **alert generation**.
    - **DarTwin Notation**: Uses a **formal language** (e.g., **UML**, **SysML**) to document **system evolution** and **change impacts** in a **machine-readable format**.
  **Key Enablers**:
  - **Reproducibility**: Entire setup is **open-source** and **documented** for **easy replication** in research/education.
  - **Modularity**: Components (e.g., controllers, sensors) are **swappable** to accommodate **different use cases** (e.g., adding a camera for vision-based control).
  - **Scalability**: Architecture supports **extension to larger systems** (e.g., multi-crane coordination) via **distributed computing** (e.g., **ROS 2**, **Kubernetes**).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (e.g., GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management) employ **decentralized, ad-hoc architectures** with **single-instance deployments** per physical system. The GreenhouseDT uses **virtual machines** for isolation, while the Air Quality Management exemplar leverages a **Raspberry Pi** as the sole physical-digital twin interface. No explicit mention of **multi-instance coordination** or hierarchical structures is provided; however, the use of **message brokers (e.g., ActiveMQ)** in GreenhouseDT suggests potential for **event-driven synchronization** between components. The **AutomationML-based digital twin** in Air Quality Management implies a **model-driven, standardized representation** that could theoretically support **multiplicity** (e.g., multiple twin instances for different zones or scenarios), but no concrete implementation details are documented. The lab-scale gantry crane exemplar does not specify multiplicities but aligns with the ad-hoc, single-instance approach observed in other cases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system exemplars adopt a **lightweight, ad-hoc orchestration pattern** without reliance on commercial frameworks. **GreenhouseDT** uses a **hybrid architecture** combining **InfluxDB (time-series database)** for data persistence, **ActiveMQ (message broker)** for asynchronous communication, and **virtual machines** for deployment, enabling **self-adaptive behavioral synchronization** between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar employs a **pipeline-based orchestration** on **Microsoft Azure**, integrating **AutomationML** for model-driven twin representation and a **Raspberry Pi** for sensor data acquisition. Both systems lack centralized orchestration engines; instead, they rely on **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines, Raspberry Pi) and **event-driven coordination** (via message brokers or direct sensor-DT links). The **gantry crane** exemplar follows a similar pattern, using **C4 Model** for architectural visualization but does not specify orchestration details beyond ad-hoc integration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems demonstrate **limited horizontal integration** with external systems, relying on **domain-specific, lightweight protocols** rather than standardized interoperability frameworks. **GreenhouseDT** integrates with **InfluxDB** (for data storage) and **ActiveMQ** (for messaging), but no explicit external system dependencies (e.g., ERP, IoT platforms) are mentioned. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, suggesting potential integration with **cloud-based services** (e.g., Azure IoT Hub, Logic Apps), but no concrete protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or enterprise system dependencies are documented. Both exemplars avoid commercial Digital Twin frameworks, opting for **ad-hoc sensor-DT links** (e.g., Raspberry Pi sensors → AutomationML twin). The **gantry crane** exemplar does not specify external integrations but implies **cyber-physical system (CPS) alignment** with logistics domains, potentially requiring **standardized CPS protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, ROS) for broader interoperability, though these are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical system (e.g., greenhouse, air quality management) and its digital twin counterpart. For **GreenhouseDT**, the system integrates **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, facilitating real-time data exchange between the physical and virtual layers. Virtual machine images are preloaded with **prerecorded data** to enable rapid deployment and testing. In the **Air Quality Management** exemplar, the digital twin is implemented using **AutomationML** for model-driven engineering, enabling structured representation of system behavior, simulation, and prediction. The system leverages **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, supporting runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics. Both exemplars adopt an **ad-hoc approach** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks, emphasizing custom model development tailored to domain-specific requirements (e.g., agricultural, construction, or logistics).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time environmental monitoring, including parameters critical for indoor air quality (e.g., particulate matter, CO₂, temperature, humidity). The **GreenhouseDT** system does not explicitly detail sensor specifications, but its reliance on **InfluxDB** suggests integration with environmental sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, light) for greenhouse control. No explicit mention of **sampling frequencies, spatial distribution, or transmission rates** is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral models** for both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, implying dynamic validation through real-time adjustments. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**, suggesting validation via **model-driven engineering (AutomationML)** and **Microsoft Azure-based analytics**. Uncertainty quantification and error correction mechanisms are not explicitly detailed, but the use of **prerecorded data in virtual machines (GreenhouseDT)** and **Azure tools (Air Quality Management)** implies structured data management for quality assurance. No explicit mention of **uncertainty sources, error detection protocols, or formal verification methods** (e.g., statistical validation, cross-model comparison) is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system is facilitated through the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program) and the **Logging Application**, which continuously transmits real-time operational data to the **TimescaleDB database**. The data types transmitted include:
- **Crane state measurements**: Position, velocity, acceleration, and orientation of the crane’s components (e.g., gantry, hoist, and puck).
- **Controller telemetry**: Sensor readings from encoders, limit switches, and other embedded systems monitoring the crane’s physical constraints.
- **Trajectory execution logs**: Timestamped records of executed movements, including waypoints, timestamps, and control signals sent to actuators.

**Communication Protocol**: The interaction relies on an unspecified protocol (not detailed in the document) between the **Physical Crane Interface** and the **Logging Application**, with data subsequently stored in **TimescaleDB** (a time-series database optimized for high-write workloads).

**Transmission Frequency**: Continuous logging implies high-frequency sampling (not explicitly quantified, but inferred to be millisecond-level for real-time control and sub-second for trajectory validation).

**Event Triggers**: Data transmission is event-driven, triggered by:
1. **State changes** (e.g., crane movement initiation/termination).
2. **Periodic polling** (implied by continuous logging).
3. **Trajectory completion** (for validation against simulated trajectories).

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Preprocessing**: The **Logging Application** likely filters or aggregates raw sensor data before storage (not explicitly detailed).
- **Validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** cross-references logged trajectories with simulated trajectories to detect anomalies (e.g., residual swinging or deviations exceeding thresholds).

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **TimescaleDB** acts as the central repository, storing raw and processed data for analytics, visualization (via **Grafana**), and validation.
- **MQTT Broker** may facilitate asynchronous messaging between services (e.g., alerting the **Dashboard** when validation thresholds are breached).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the Digital Twin system is mediated by the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service**, which translate digital commands into physical actions for the **Physical Crane**. Key aspects include:

**Command Types**:
- **Trajectory commands**: Generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service** using a kinematics model to produce optimal movement profiles (e.g., spline-based paths) with zero residual swinging at termination.
- **Configuration commands**: Sent via the **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** to adjust crane parameters (e.g., speed limits, safety zones).
- **Emergency stop commands**: Triggered manually or via the **Continuous Validation Service** if physical-virtual discrepancies exceed thresholds.

**Control Message Formats**:
- **Structured payloads** (likely JSON or binary) containing:
  - **Waypoints**: Cartesian or joint-space coordinates.
  - **Timestamps**: For synchronized execution.
  - **Velocity/acceleration profiles**: Derived from the kinematics model.
- **Command validation**: The **Control Application** verifies trajectory feasibility against physical constraints (e.g., crane limits) before transmission.

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- **Trajectory validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories (logged data) with simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**) using distance metrics (e.g., Euclidean error). Threshold breaches trigger alerts via the **Dashboard**.
- **Authentication**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied for critical commands (e.g., HMI-to-Control Application communication).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Real-time telemetry**: The **Physical Crane Interface** relays execution feedback (e.g., actual vs. commanded position) to the **Logging Application** and **Validation Service**.
- **Conflict resolution**: Not explicitly described, but the **Control Application** likely prioritizes commands based on urgency (e.g., emergency stops override trajectory commands).

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- **Acknowledgment signals**: The **Physical Crane Interface** confirms receipt/execution of commands (mechanism unspecified but inferred via logging).
- **Post-execution validation**: The **Validation Service** generates alerts if discrepancies exceed thresholds, enabling corrective action via the **Dashboard**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical network infrastructure** is designed to support real-time interaction between physical and virtual components, with the following specifications:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **MQTT**: Used as a lightweight, publish-subscribe broker for inter-service signaling (e.g., alerting the **Dashboard** of validation failures or trajectory completion).
- **Database protocols**: **TimescaleDB** supports high-throughput writes (optimized for time-series data) and likely uses PostgreSQL’s native protocols for client-server communication.
- **Physical interface protocols**: Unspecified, but the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python-based) likely uses serial (e.g., RS-232/485), Ethernet/IP, or proprietary protocols to communicate with crane controllers.

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Centralized**: Services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **Validation Service**) communicate via the **MQTT Broker** and **TimescaleDB**, implying a star topology with the database as a central hub.
- **Service isolation**: Containers (e.g., **Control Application**, **Logging Application**) are logically separated but interconnected via shared data stores and messaging.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Low-latency paths**: Critical for real-time control (e.g., trajectory execution) and validation (e.g., comparing logged vs. simulated data). Latency targets are not quantified but inferred to be <100ms for control loops.
- **High-throughput logging**: **TimescaleDB** must handle continuous writes from the **Logging Application** (unspecified volume but likely kilobytes/second for sensor data).

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **Database redundancy**: **TimescaleDB** may include replication or backups (not detailed).
- **Service resilience**: The **Validation Service** and **Dashboard** rely on continuous data streams; failures may trigger alerts but no explicit failover is described.
- **MQTT QoS**: Likely configured for at-least-once delivery to ensure critical alerts (e.g., threshold breaches) are not lost.

**Security Protocols**:
- **Not explicitly detailed**, but implied measures may include:
  - **Authentication**: For HMI access or critical command paths.
  - **Encryption**: For data in transit (e.g., MQTT over TLS, database connections).
  - **Access control**: Role-based (e.g., **Operator** vs. **Researcher** access to the **Dashboard**).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s hosting and deployment infrastructure** is tailored to the lab-scale gantry crane system, with the following technical specifics:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **On-premise**: The system is deployed locally (implied by the lab-scale context and lack of cloud references), likely on a single machine or small cluster for low-latency operation.
- **Edge deployment**: The **Physical Crane Interface** and **Control Application** run on hardware co-located with the crane to minimize latency.

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **Database**: **TimescaleDB** requires sufficient RAM and disk I/O for time-series data storage (unspecified capacity but optimized for high-write workloads).
- **Services**:
  - **Trajectory Generator**: Computationally intensive (kinematics modeling) but likely lightweight for lab-scale operations.
  - **Simulation Service**: Requires real-time processing for trajectory validation.
  - **MQTT Broker**: Low-resource overhead for local messaging.
- **Frontend**: The **Dashboard (Grafana)** and **HMI** are likely hosted on the same machine or a separate workstation for operator interaction.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Vertical scaling**: Implied for the database (e.g., increasing RAM/disk for larger datasets).
- **Service modularity**: Containers (e.g., **Validation Service**) can be scaled independently if deployed in a containerized environment (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes), though not explicitly detailed.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Monolithic or microservices**: The C4 container diagram suggests a microservices-like architecture (e.g., **Trajectory Generator** as a standalone service), but deployment specifics (e.g., orchestration) are not described.
- **Hybrid**: Physical components (crane hardware) are standalone, while digital twin services are software-based and co-located.

**Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
- **Continuous operation**: Critical services (e.g., **Logging Application**, **Validation Service**) must run persistently to ensure real-time data capture and validation.
- **Fault tolerance**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied redundancy in data storage (e.g., **TimescaleDB** backups) and service monitoring (e.g., **Dashboard** alerts).
- **Maintenance**: The system is designed for research and operational use, with no explicit high-availability (HA) clustering or disaster recovery (DR) mechanisms described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management) utilize an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than relying on a commercial digital twin framework. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **virtual machines (VMs)** with pre-recorded data for ease of installation, leveraging **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** as a message broker for communication between components. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates a **Raspberry Pi** with embedded sensors for real-time data acquisition and an **AutomationML-based digital twin** for model-driven engineering. Both systems emphasize **containerization or virtualization technologies** (e.g., VMs) to facilitate dissemination and reproducibility of the codebase. **Version control** is implied via GitHub hosting ([GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)), though explicit versioning strategies (e.g., semantic versioning, branching models) are not detailed. **Quality assurance** and **testing** are not explicitly described, but the use of pre-recorded data in GreenhouseDT suggests offline validation capabilities. **Evolution management** is inferred to be iterative, given the focus on self-adaptation (GreenhouseDT) and model-driven engineering (Air Quality Management), though no formal methodologies (e.g., Agile, DevOps) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides three key **decision support capabilities**:
  1. **Runtime visualization** of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter) via sensor data from a **Raspberry Pi**,
  2. **Simulation** of air flow dynamics and pollutant dispersion using an **AutomationML-based digital twin**, and
  3. **Prediction** of virus spread risks through data-driven analytics.
  The system leverages **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, implying cloud-based analytics (e.g., Azure Machine Learning, Stream Analytics) for processing time-series sensor data. **User interaction** is not detailed, but runtime visualization suggests dashboard-based interfaces. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are not specified, though the use of **AutomationML** (a standardized engineering format) implies structured, reproducible models. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **self-adaptation** of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, though no explicit decision-making processes or analytics are described beyond behavioral feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adheres to **AutomationML** ([ISO 15926-compliant](https://www.automationml.org/)) for digital twin modeling, ensuring interoperability with industrial engineering tools. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly mention standardization but relies on **open-source technologies** (InfluxDB, ActiveMQ) and **virtualization** (VMs), which are widely adopted in IoT and CPS domains. Neither exemplar references **certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO 23247 for Digital Twins, IEC 62443 for cybersecurity). **Interoperability** is limited to internal toolchains (e.g., AutomationML for Air Quality Management), with no evidence of cross-domain standards (e.g., OPC UA, FIWARE) for broader integration.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)



















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
